dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for chest pain and possible cardiac issue.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I started having sharp chest pain rated 8 out of 10, and it radiates around my chest.
You mentioned it worsens when you stand or exert yourself. Any shortness of breath?
Patient is alert and oriented x4 with appropriate eye tracking.
Eyes are open spontaneously and follow objects with appropriate tracking.
Patient responds appropriately to questions and gives accurate details about symptoms.
Testing motor response now. Squeeze my fingers on demand. Appropriate response on both sides.
Airway is patent with no signs of obstruction or abnormal sounds.
Breathing appears unlabored but rapid. Let's confirm full assessment.
Respirations are 27 with increased effort. No cyanosis observed.
Bilateral breath sounds heard, though some wheezing reported.
Radial pulses are absent at this time.
Skin is cool, clammy, and ashen in tone.
Reassessing AVPU. Full orientation to person, place, time, and situation.
You described a sudden onset of sharp chest pain. It does not sound like tearing or crushing.
No relief with rest; pain gets worse when you stand.
You haven't skipped food or drink in the last three hours and drank just water recently.
You mentioned a history of heart failure. Any AICD or implanted devices?
I have an LVAD. No recent alarms or changes.
When did your chest pain begin? Around 30 minutes ago. Were you doing anything at the time?
You said it gets worse when you stand. Do you find it easier to lie down?
Describe the pain. Sharp with no specific burning sensations noted.
It wraps around your chest. You're not having left arm or jaw pain at this time.
You're rating the pain at 8 out of 10 and it has not improved with rest or activity.
You've had the pain for roughly 30 minutes. Has it waxed or waned at all?
Skin remains ashen, clammy, with no observable rashes.
Pupils are equal and reactive, 3mm without signs of head trauma.
No nasal congestion or injury. No visible airway compromise.
Normal chest rise with equal breath sounds. Still note wheezing bilaterally.
No guarding or pain reported when palpating the abdomen or chest.
No pain or distention reported.
Equal motion and sensation in all four limbs. No swelling or numbness noted.
Pulse rate is 98 with regular rhythm.
Respiratory rate remains at 27 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 89 over 63 millimeters of mercury.
SpO2 reads 97 percent with no supplemental O2 used.
Attempting to obtain blood glucose; could not get a reading on the first try.
Sinus rhythm present with heavy artifact likely due to the LVAD.
We are performing capnography now to assess CO2 levels.
Applying four-lead ECG now. Signal quality appears challenged.
12-lead ECG on file. Discordant T-waves noted.
Establishing IV access via the external jugular.
Administering 150 mL of normal saline to support blood pressure.
Placing you in a semi-Fowler's position to maximize comfort and breathing efficiency.
Positioned for transport with shoulder and lap belts secured.
Continue ECG and capnography monitoring in the ambulance.
Repeat vital readings and symptom checks to be done frequently during transport.
We are heading to VCU Health Systems ED for further evaluation and care.
No immediate changes in symptoms. Pain remains localized and 8/10 in intensity.
Vitals still show 98 bpm, 27 respirations, 89/63 BP, 97 SpO2.
Level of pain is the same as reported before. It is still discreet and not spreading.
Vital signs and readings are unchanged since the last standing check.
Onset of any new symptoms will be assessed on the fly if reported.
Skin and breathing efforts remain consistent except for artifact on the EKG trace.
IV line is patent with no current infiltration or complications noted.
Arrived at VCU Medical Center. ED-R Bed 8 is awaiting us.
Updates on all vitals and medical history provided to ED team directly on arrival.
Patient protocol followed under 2-1 cardiac suspected per incident response.
Final EKG trace sinus rhythm with some electrical interference from the LVAD.
Patient has now been transferred for further evaluation. Vitals are documented.
Last recorded pain is still 8/10, no improvement reported with rest.
Oxygen saturation rate still at 97%, same EKG rhythm.
No new changes in condition. No additional interventions required.
EPC documentation complete and sent to system.
Patient denies any new symptoms after final on-scene scan.
Documentation published and accepted after physical handover.
Recent assessment confirms no deterioration from preceding normal status.
Pre-arrival call placed with incoming details to ED-R Bed 8 on VCUHS.
Position adjusted once during transit as needed.
EKG maintenance and monitoring kept stable in transit.
No noticeable changes since EMA entry and last notes.
Motion and sensation remain stable before exit.
BBS bilat with no new changes reported in transit.
Passed 60-minute mark with no issues from front to back and bed to bed.
IV site stable apart from minor issues during transport.
Basic outputs appear stable with no progressive deterioration.
Nothing added anew in transit post-second narrative collection.
All medicine regimen remains consistent.
No new meds or field-assisted adjustment or change.
No peanut exposure occurred today. Allergies management appears stable.
No drug-seeking behaviors observed or new medicine records identified.
Last valid intake within 3 hours is confirmed.
Describe the moment pain began. I collapsed on hands and knees in the kitchen.
No recent orthopedic falls or trips led up to incident.
LVAD functioning well per patient's own claims.
Reassessing overall status mid-transit to ensure continued stability.
EKG waveform stable with no new anomalies on record.
No extremity changes in status during the remainder of transport.
No signs necessitate further testing or intervention at this point.
All systems remain clear before unloading on arrival.
Documentation published and accepted after physical handover.
Patient internally accepted upon arrival to ED-R Bed 8.
Patient accepted now in ED, no delays or false identification with onset time.
EPC packet transferred in person, all details correct and matched.
Condition well-represented for admission to ER and transfer to internal medicine team.
Patient is now under ED care; no false admission or oversight expected.
All pre-hospital data aligned with current institutional evaluation and management.
Final handover completed without issues or further interventions.
On arrival, final pass with ED bed set and provided good patient turnover.
Done. Patient care transferred; Vitals and medical background correctly reported.
